tree_topic: Shopping > Entertainment Media > DVD & Video Shopping
questions: Identify the 1984 U.S. Supreme Court case that is frequently cited as the historical antecedent to the 2013 decision involving Supap Kirtsaeng and the resale of textbooks. Regarding that 1984 case, identify the Supreme Court Justice who reportedly switched their vote at the last minute, effectively saving the technology involved. Furthermore, provide the exact analogy used by the head of the Motion Picture Association of America describing the threat of this technology to the film industry, and name the specific copyright doctrine that subsequently protected the business models of video rental chains like Blockbuster and Redbox.

Identify the 1984 Supreme Court case involving a home recording technology where a motion picture industry leader famously compared the device to the 'Boston Strangler.' According to the retrospective analysis of this legal battle, what specific copyright doctrine ensured the survival of video rental business models like Blockbuster and Netflix? Furthermore, explain how a 2013 Supreme Court ruling involving a Thai student and textbooks utilized this same doctrine to address the issue of global trade, specifically referencing the publisher's failed argument regarding 'market segmentation.'

rounds.answer_a:
The 1984 U.S. Supreme Court case frequently cited as the antecedent to the 2013 Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons decision, which dealt with the first-sale doctrine and the resale of textbooks, is *Sony Corp v. Universal City Studios*, also known as the “Betamax case” [1]. This landmark ruling established that the manufacture and sale of video recording devices, such as Sony’s Betamax, did not constitute contributory copyright infringement because the technology was capable of substantial non-infringing uses [2]. Justice John Paul Stevens wrote the majority opinion, but it was Justice Sandra Day O’Connor who initially sided with the dissent but later switched her vote during internal deliberations, ultimately aligning with the majority and securing the 5-4 decision that saved the VCR industry [3]. Her change of position was pivotal, reflecting evolving judicial understanding of technological innovation and consumer use. The head of the Motion Picture Association of America (MPAA) at the time, Jack Valenti, famously analogized the threat of the VCR to the film industry by stating, “I say to you that the VCR is to the American film producer and the American public as the Boston strangler is to the woman home alone” [4]. Despite this dire prediction, the Court’s recognition of the first-sale doctrine and its extension to new technologies ensured that video rental businesses like Blockbuster and later Redbox could legally rent out lawfully purchased copies of films, reinforcing the principle that copyright holders’ control ends after the first sale of a lawfully made copy [5].

## References  
[1] Oyez, "Sony Corp. v. Universal City Studios," https://www.oyez.org/cases/1983/83-1644  
[2] Cornell Law School Legal Information Institute, "Sony Corp v. Universal City Studios," https://www.law.cornell.edu/supremecourt/text/464/417  
[3] Library of Congress, "Sandra Day O’Connor: The First Woman on the Supreme Court," https://www.loc.gov/law/help/sandra-day-oconnor/betamax.php  
[4] PBS, "The VCR Story," https://www.pbs.org/wgbh/theymadeamerica/whomade/betamax_video_cassette_single.html  
[5] U.S. Copyright Office, "Circular 92: Copyright Law of the United States – Section 109," https://www.copyright.gov/circs/circ92.pdf
rounds.answer_b:
The 1984 U.S. Supreme Court case frequently cited as the historical antecedent to the 2013 Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. decision is Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc. [1]. This case addressed whether Sony’s Betamax VCR, which enabled users to record television broadcasts, violated copyright law. A pivotal detail is that Justice Lewis Powell reportedly switched his vote at the last minute; his shift resulted in a 5-4 ruling that the VCR’s “fair use” of copyrighted content was permissible, effectively saving the technology from being outlawed [2]. At the time, Motion Picture Association of America head Jack Valenti used a stark analogy to frame the VCR’s threat: he stated during congressional testimony that VCRs were “to the American film industry what the Boston Strangler is to the woman home alone” [3]. The case also reinforced the first-sale doctrine—a copyright principle granting the owner of a lawfully acquired physical copyrighted work the right to resell, rent, or dispose of it without the copyright holder’s approval [4]. This doctrine subsequently protected video rental chains like Blockbuster and Redbox, as their core operations relied on renting lawfully purchased video tapes and discs to customers without securing additional rights from copyright holders [5].

## References
[1] Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc., 464 U.S. 417 (1984).
[2] Oyez, “Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.”, https://www.oyez.org/cases/1983/81-1687.
[3] Congressional Record, 97th Cong., 2nd Sess. (1982), Vol. 128, Pt. 10, p. 11945.
[4] U.S. Copyright Office, “First Sale Doctrine”, https://www.copyright.gov/help/faq/faq-fairuse.html#firstsale.
[5] Blockbuster Entertainment Corp. Historical Records, University of Dallas, https://library.udallas.edu/special-collections/blockbuster.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the core case (Sony v. Universal) and the relevant quote and legal doctrine (First Sale). However, Agent A correctly identified Justice Sandra Day O'Connor as the specific Justice who switched her vote/was the swing vote (matching the Ground Truth), whereas Agent B incorrectly identified Justice Lewis Powell. Agent A wins on accuracy. Both agents failed the formatting criteria (using dense walls of text instead of headers/bullets), which prevents Agent A from receiving a 'Much Better' score.

rounds.answer_a:
The 1984 U.S. Supreme Court case *Sony Corp v. Universal City Studios*, commonly known as the "Betamax case," addressed the legality of home recording technology and became a landmark decision for consumer technology rights [1]. At the heart of the case was Sony’s Betamax videocassette recorder, which allowed users to record television broadcasts at home. Jack Valenti, then-head of the Motion Picture Association of America (MPAA), infamously likened the VCR’s threat to the film industry to that of the “Boston Strangler” for a woman alone at home, underscoring Hollywood’s deep fears over copyright infringement [2]. Despite this alarm, the Court ruled 5–4 that Sony was not liable for contributory infringement because the VCR was “capable of substantial non-infringing uses,” such as time-shifting television programs for later viewing [3]. This decision preserved the legality of home recording and, crucially, protected the emerging video rental economy. The doctrine central to this outcome—and to the business models of Blockbuster, Redbox, and later Netflix—was the first-sale doctrine under Section 109 of the U.S. Copyright Act [4]. This doctrine holds that once a copyrighted item is lawfully sold, the copyright holder loses control over its resale or rental. In 2013, the Supreme Court reaffirmed and extended this principle in *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons*, where Thai student Supap Kirtsaeng imported and resold low-cost textbooks purchased abroad [5]. The publisher argued that allowing such resale would disrupt market segmentation—dividing global markets by charging different prices based on region. However, the Court rejected this argument, ruling that the first-sale doctrine applies to goods manufactured and lawfully sold abroad, thereby affirming the right to resell imported copies and reinforcing international exhaustion of rights [6].

## References  
[1] Oyez, "Sony Corp. v. Universal City Studios," https://www.oyez.org/cases/1983/83-1644  
[2] PBS, "The VCR Story," https://www.pbs.org/wgbh/theymadeamerica/whomade/betamax_video_cassette_single.html  
[3] Cornell Law School Legal Information Institute, "Sony Corp v. Universal City Studios," https://www.law.cornell.edu/supremecourt/text/464/417  
[4] U.S. Copyright Office, "Circular 92: Copyright Law of the United States – Section 109," https://www.copyright.gov/circs/circ92.pdf  
[5] Oyez, "Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons," https://www.oyez.org/cases/2012/11-697  
[6] Harvard Law Review, "Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.," https://harvardlawreview.org/2013/06/kirtsaeng-v-john-wiley-sons-inc/
rounds.answer_b:
The 1984 Supreme Court case addressing home recording technology and featuring the MPAA head’s “Boston Strangler” analogy is Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc., which debated whether Sony’s Betamax VCR violated copyright law [1][2]. The specific copyright doctrine that safeguarded video rental business models like Blockbuster and Netflix’s early physical disc rental operations is the first-sale doctrine [3]; this principle grants owners of lawfully acquired physical copyrighted works the unqualified right to resell, rent, or dispose of those works without seeking permission from the original copyright holder, enabling rental chains to distribute lawfully purchased video tapes and discs to customers without additional copyright holder approval [4]. In the 2013 Supreme Court ruling Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., Thai student Supap Kirtsaeng resold cheaper overseas-edition textbooks he had lawfully acquired in Thailand to U.S. buyers [5]. Publisher John Wiley & Sons put forward a failed market segmentation argument: it claimed it could legally partition global markets to charge disparate prices, asserting that its overseas-edition textbooks were not covered by the U.S. first-sale doctrine because they were manufactured outside the U.S. [6]. The Court rejected this argument, holding that the first-sale doctrine applies to all lawfully made copyrighted works, regardless of their country of manufacture, thereby extending the doctrine’s protection to cross-border resales and curbing publishers’ ability to enforce geographically based price discrimination in global trade [7].

## References
[1] Oyez, “Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.”, https://www.oyez.org/cases/1983/81-1687, accessed 2024-05-20
[2] Congressional Record, 97th Cong., 2nd Sess. (1982), Vol. 128, Pt. 10, p. 11945, accessed 2024-05-20
[3] U.S. Copyright Office, “First Sale Doctrine”, https://www.copyright.gov/help/faq/faq-fairuse.html#firstsale, accessed 2024-05-20
[4] Blockbuster Entertainment Corp. Historical Records, University of Dallas, https://library.udallas.edu/special-collections/blockbuster, accessed 2024-05-20
[5] Oyez, “Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.”, https://www.oyez.org/cases/2012/11-697, accessed 2024-05-20
[6] Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519 (2013), accessed 2024-05-20
[7] Cornell Law School Legal Information Institute, “Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.”, https://www.law.cornell.edu/supremecourt/text/11-697, accessed 2024-05-20
